original_abstract: Scientific integrity serves as the backbone of evidence-based policymaking, particularly during global crises when public trust in experts can determine the success of emergency interventions. Despite recent concerns that the epistemic authority of science is eroding, empirical evidence regarding global trust levels remains fragmented. In this study, we addressed these concerns through a pre-registered survey of 71,922 respondents across 68 countries. Our findings reveal that, contrary to popular narratives of widespread skepticism, the majority of people worldwide trust scientists and support their active involvement in societal and political discourse. While trust levels are generally high, we identify significant variations driven by individual and country-level factors, such as political orientation and socioeconomic context. Although a wholesale "crisis of trust" is not evident, even a localized lack of confidence among a small minority can hinder the integration of scientific evidence into public policy. These results offer critical insights for scientists and policymakers striving to bolster the social contract between science and the public.
humanized_abstract: Scientific integrity is often seen as an essential ingredient in what has come to be referred to as “evidence-based policy-making”, especially during global emergencies. Whether managing an infectious pandemic, a global financial crisis or climate change there is often an immediate window in which expert advice from scientists and their organisations can have a material impact and where the populations to be advised may be receptive to interventions in the emergency phase of a crisis. Indeed there has been an increasing realisation that the epistemic authority of science may be under threat; but there appears to have been no systematic assessment of what global citizens think about the role of scientists or the extent to which there has been a crisis of trust at a global level. Our paper, drawing on a large pre-registered survey of 71,922 individuals in 68 countries provides a direct assessment of levels of trust across countries. We find that overall citizens in most countries trust scientists and also think that scientists should play more active roles in society and in politics. However there is considerable heterogeneity in trust both at the level of the individual and of countries; and we examine the determinants and implications of trust and consider both a “crisis of trust” narrative and an alternative “harm from the mean” account where while a global “crisis of trust” may not have occurred trust in scientists among even in otherwise trusting populations can be too low to ensure that there is an adequate transmission of scientific-based information to national policymakers to affect policy choice, which is a critical component of the “social contract” between science, the scientists and the citizenry of a country.
